author: @mwmentor
text: Very interesting - I live in a warmer climate and I have not found a successful evaporation cooler yet... maybe I am just not looking at the right products :-(  At the risk of being pedantic, the capillary action does not pull the water up the stem of a tree or anywhere else - the fluid, regardless of what it is is actually pushed upwards as the pressure drop from the leaves in a tree (or the vacuum effect of us sucking fluid through a straw) takes effect.  The atmosphere is heavy, thus the fluid is pushed and not pulled.  Told you it was pedantic - it is correct though.  Thanks for sharing and have a great day :-)

author: @davidmowbray6352
text: I've seen a nomadic cool box made with lamb's fleece cover which is wetted and riding horse speeds up evaporation and really cools the box down.

author: @williambianchi2006
text:
For those in areas with high humidity where a zeer pot or swamp cooler won't work well, it is possible to run a freezer on solar and a battery. It is an expense to set up for that, but it will work. From there, you can meet refrigeration needs with frozen gel packs and a cooler.
This would also open the door for personal AC experiments, personal meaning cool air blown on a person and not cooling a whole room. I've been wondering if a frozen sand battery would work to cool air blown by a fan and if that effect would last longer than an equivalent sized block of ice. (At least the sand shouldn't leave a big puddle of water when used)  Maybe 3 or 4 sand batteries in the freezer and rotate as needed? A gallon of sand has more mass, or weighs more, than a gallon of water, so perhaps the cooling effect would last longer. Won't know until I try.
Then, there are experiments with getting water from air. Cold water in a styrofoam cooler circulated through coiled copper outside the cooler, then back into the cooler should cause moisture in the air to condense of the copper coils for as long as the water in the cooler stays cold. Ice trays in the freezer would keep that operation producing. Just refill the trays with water from the cooler to freeze them again. Or, drop a frozen sand battery in the cooler to keep that water cold.
If electricity becomes scarce or unaffordable, we will need to use different methods in our day to day lives. Robert mentions this frequently and I think he is right about that being a possibility in our near future.

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
Capillary action is based on the atmospheric pressure at the top of the column and not the head pressure of the standing water tube.

I believe that this is caused by electrostatic attraction between ions in the water and ions in the air.

I would enjoy a comparison between deionized water and a simple electrolyte to see what the difference might be.

author: @uksuperrascal
text: A Question To All - Do you leave you washing machine plugged in and on stand by  24 7 ?  -  I just measured my Washing Machine stand by  volts and current and was amazed that when the machine was in stand by it measured 243 V AC @ 1.5 amps = 364.5 Watts. So my machine is always turned off when not in use - My Machine get used may be 3 time a week. so that = about 90 mins @ 225 V @ 7A when in heat cycle = 1K575 Watts and the rest of the time 330 mins @ 225 V @ 5 A = 1K125 Watts - just by switching off my washing machine after each use, I save £135 a Year.

author: @ghosttheoremproductions5469
text: Getting cold from dry. As no amount of heat gets you evaporation if the air is already saturated with water vapor.There are swamp cooler designs which are pumpless but their cooling potential is severely decreased. Even here in the desert we use pumped versions. The biggest issue is that the hottest areas are either the moistest or the driest. The moistest areas have already saturated air so no evaporative cooling takes place. The driest places consume large amount of water which is a scarce resource in those environment. Or, you can have the worst of both worlds when we hit monsoon here in the desert. It's a tech that has been used by humans for thousands of years but it is extremely limited in scope and capability. - Earth Tubes may be the only environment-agnostic cooling option which "actually" achieves cold from hot. As warm air rises through the structure and is expelled the system draws fresh ambient air into the tubes. That air then gives up its heat energy to the surrounding earth which is sub-ambient. By the time the fresh air makes it into the structure it is sub-ambient as well. Essentially using the earth's thermal mass as a giant heat sink for your inbound air. Also, higher humidity actually increases the performance as more water vapor makes for more thermally conductive air.

author: @gotbread2
text: I have been working on a device very similar to this for a while now. It works similar to your sketch at 5:34, however it uses outside air as inlet, and expells the humid air back to the outside. As a net result, this device is able to cool down the water which circulates in a cooling loop and then into a radiator. Currently it can cool down a bulk of water 3-4°C below room temperature, enough to be useful on a hot day. It does so while consuming only ~24W for pump, air blower and radiator fans. It is still a prototype, but the concept works well. One can even use a multi stage design to achive even lower temperatures

replies.author: @gotbread2
replies.text: SaltyBrains It literally is not, since the device stands here in my room during the summer. I have not measured how much thermal power it can remove, but keeping the water and air circulating does not take much power. all the "work" is done by the evaporation of water. It currently is attached to a radiator with some fans to cool the room air. Its not doing that much since with only 3-4°C difference you wont get much heat transfer, but it is noticeable if you are close. definitely a nice to have in the summer.

author: @LOFIGSD

replies.text: @Noodle999 not really, you just trap the air in the attic, my neighbours do that, it just builds up, their upstairs will be over 40C, overnight and mine will be 25 max, because the heat has somewhere to go and drags the air underneath out the house

replies.author: @Noodle999
replies.text:
@LOFIGSD It's a lot to do with relative temperature and when you open it. Our loft is ventilated so once it's cooler outside than it is inside, I open the loft hatch and the bi-fold doors downstairs and it shifts a fair bit of air through.

Then later I close the loft to promote airflow through the rooms instead of it going straight out through the roof.

replies.author: @DoctorMangler
replies.text: You can get around this in a way, and still be more efficient than regular AC.  The cooler humid air is run over heat exchangers in 2 or 3 stages, using the dry cooler incoming air in each stage.  There used to be at least two commercial units that worked this way but they obviously need more parts and more blower capacity.  Supposedly they work to both reduce the final temp below what a single stage unit can do and approach ac temps in dry climates while still saving some energy over conventional ac.  The key is not running cool humid air back through the system, the heat must be transferred through an exchanger to keep the incoming air at climate humidity.

replies.author: @kennickel878
replies.text: Liquid desiccant are bacteriostatic and stator fans can handle significant back pressure…a single pass system could have a paper towel filter behind a cyclonic bucket intake (96% removal 1 micron). If the exchanger itself knocks down another 50% you’re looking at a hospital grade air handler that can over pressure a room in the event of a wildfire while keeping occupants relatively cool as well. Slap a charcoal filter on the outlet and have more capable air handlers than the Maginot line. Be sure to oversize tubing and filters to reduce restriction.

author: @technicalfool
text:
Problem with these approaches is the humidity that swamp coolers generate. They kinda-sorta work and use a lot less energy than traditional aircon and heat pump systems, but when you live on an island walking distance away from tidal waters and a bike ride from the coast, evaporative room coolers are problematic.

Personally I gave up looking for ways to cool the room, and went for personal cooling instead. Blue-gel freezer block, wrap in a tea-towel to prevent freezer burns, keep pressed against skin via clothing or just cuddling it like a cold-water bottle. Lasts for hours at a time and absolutely helped take the edge off.

Also while poking around for a few weeks before the inevitable Summer heatwave, I did come across phase-change or PCM-cooled clothing that promises to maintain 15 or 22C after just a short dip in chilled water or a small stop in the freezer. Be interesting if there's a way of doing that without spending £175 on a jacket meant to be worn under fursuits.

author: @buffplums
text:
I also love the idea used in desert areas to cool homes by drawing air out from inside the house through holes in a chimney. As the air moves past the holes in the chimney, it causes a drop in air pressure in the chimney, air is sucked in to the house at ground level which then blows over a tiled floor which in turn removes heat which then escapes up,the chimney. 
Meant to be quite an effective form of cooling. Depends upon a good airflow though.

author: @peterclark6290
text:
The water channels in trees (xylem) have a nett negative charge. H²O has a nett positive charge. So effectively (well, perhaps as a visual aid) the water molecules tumble up the xylem.
One problem with the concept in this video: evaporation raises humidity which in itself raises human discomfort. So for cooling human spaces some mechanical assistance and separation will be necessary.

author: @bayoubuddha8048
text: I once had a aquarium water filter that used no power. Gravity would allow it to syphon the water up hill, so to speak to the filter hanging on the top of the aquarium and then gravity would allow said clean water to trickle back into the aquarium. I often wondered how it worked and both the maximum distance the water could be manipulated to flow above the main body of water as well as the maximum flow... gallon per minute.  Any assistance in understanding this would be deeply appreciated, as I'm wanting to have a water flow from the back lake too the house without using any power. off grid and self feeding so to speak.

replies.author: @zootednBootedG
replies.text: Sounds like your looking for a trompe pump? Water ram? Peace and freedom ✌

replies.author: @bayoubuddha8048
replies.text: @zootednBootedG A Tromp would be huge for compressed air, but I've never heard of a tromp pump. The water ram only works with running water. my lake is fed thru underwater springs and I'm below sea lvl with no high cliff nor running water. only a small lake in the back yard. I'm hoping to have the water flow over the roof during the summer to assist in cooling, with the run off returning back into the lake

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate but there is not enough info there - a syphon wouldn't do that as we all know so there must have been some part of the system you didn't notice

replies.author: @Akawaki
replies.text: @bayoubuddha8048 You could excavate a section to get the waterram lower

author: @nonyabusiness9747
text: Really appreciate this one because I am building a wallapini and I have a pool of water at the bottom of it I am wondering what to do with. Naturally my first thought was to pump it out. Then I thought I can pump it to the top of a planted wall and it just dribble back down. But with this knowledge I could place some of this florist foam in the water and help to further cool the greenhouse.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I might use good old paper mate - it truly is amazing and 'greener' - which seems good for a green house lol

author: @smob0
text: I did some looking into greenhouse designs and one interesting cooler I seen was similar to this. It was basically what you had there, only the black piece on top was a black piece of fabric, and they drapped the fabric down one side of the container and had a fan blowing on the fabric (also it was much larger). My only problem with swamp coolers is they make the air more humid, and are less efficient in high humidity. Unfortunately where I live, heat and humidity are hand in hand, so they aren't a silver bullet to heat problems here,  but I'm still playing with them in my greenhouse.

replies.author: @misamsung6191
replies.text: Rather than use the most cloth running.  A better design is to pump cold water through a length of copper pipe, neoprene tubing or a finned radiator with a fan blowing over it.  You can recirculate the cold water through an ice chest full of ice.  It works a treat.  Although you are limited by how long the cooler will keep the ice and ice water cold.

replies.author: @brendanwarrick4978
replies.text: Yep, that design your referring to Dan is actually commercially used to increase humidity in a greenhouse. As long as there is air flow, plants actually prefer 60- 80 humility on average. Especially tropical

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a shame because in dry areas they are awesome - but I am thinking about the humid areas and I have an idea or two

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Get 4 or more poles 3 meters long mount them vertically next to the greenhouse in a square or circular pattern 60 cm apart in a place you can put a vent hole at the top of the greenhouse wrap the poles in black sheet plastic and leave the top open to the sky and a vent controlled pipe from the greenhouse to the black tube. the sun will heat the tube and draw hot air from your greenhouse like a vacuum.

author: @rwells3376
text: The problem you have with this is the humidity that is caused by it. In Arizona where I lived a few years back we used these all of the time in most every application. There the humidity is actually beneficial, but in most areas it destroys everything, rust, mildew, mold. Maybe if we could affix it to a heat sink and draw air over or thru it, and contain the humid air then maybe it could be used.

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
I'm not so sure about this being a problem!! (No disrespect!)
Due to the Old very damp house my flat is in, I've got to use my AC  and dehumidifier unit at the same time during summer....

On paper, this means that I only have to use the dehumidifier part of the  system..

So I don't need to use the AC part quite as much!! 
And if I am able to Wick up the water from it and using towards cooling the place down.

I'd be able to do this, even if there's anything like the hosepipe ban....
Without being fined during drought conditions!!
Or causing environmental lack.

So far I have just got thinking about this, but it's something that I am going to tinkering with definitely!!

Even if I may have to get the water and physically shift it ,
part of the way I would be able to save a few Quid, 
and make my carbon footprint smaller than before...

Any thoughts on this please?!
If you are able to see glaring holes in this, please let me know. .TIA.
Andrea and Critters. ...XxX....

replies.author: @brendanwarrick4978
replies.text: Maybe a desiccant in-line would be a great way to combating that. It would lower the humidity of that room

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
@brendanwarrick4978 It's the whole house!! Three floors and I am the only tenant left..
Due to the landlord selling up, I'm a sitting Tenant for now, Still!!
I do get your point though, and I would be happy to hear about your thoughts about desiccants?!
They'd have to be absolutely safe for animals and birds, as I rescue rehab and release wildlife mainly.
And I have my best friend, Jasper, who's my support assist dog,(Retired!) 
I've got to admit that I have no idea about desiccants, and I apologise for my lack of knowledge!!
Namaste 🙏
Andrea and Critters. ...XxX....

replies.author: @brendanwarrick4978
replies.text: @AndreaDingbatt there are lots of different desiccant, have a look into it because they cost no electricity and slowly revolves moisture from the air. You can do a large area but you just need fresh air from outside passes desiccant before the area your trying to dehumidifie

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't know about most areas mate - in dry conditions it is ideal and in humid conditions not so much that is very true - but as Brendan said just stick a desiccant in line = however I am thinking about the problem and will do videos on it

author: @johnharrison373
text: In the 50's and 60's (maybe before) you could get a bottle cooler made of terracotta.  You'd soke it in water overnight and when the milkman delivered your daily pint, he'd place it over the top of the milk bottle. In summer in the sun, it would actually freeze the milk.

replies.author: @sumdumbmick
replies.text: congratulations. too bad everyone got lead poisoning and became mentally retarded immediately after that. just to make the engines run a tiny bit better.

replies.author: @metralla
replies.text: There is a traditional terracota bottle in Spain called "botijo" that follows the same principle. The terracota is porous so the bottle "sweats" and cools the water inside.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text: That reminds me of a Zeer pot. Two terracotta pots placed one inside the other and a layer of dirt in between.  Water the dirt, the outside pot evaporates the water, and the inside pit remains cool for storing perishables
